section.title: Basic objects
section.p: The following definitions clarify and expand the definitions given in .
The following is an amendment to Definition 4.1.4 on p.66 of .

definition.p: Let A,B be sets. A relation from A to B is a subset R\subseteq A\times B of the Cartesian product A\times B. A relation from a set A to itself is called a relation on the set A. Given a relation R from A to B, the inverse relation to R, denoted R^{-1}, is the relation from B to A given by R^{-1} = \{(b,a)\colon (a,b)\in \R\}.

section.p: The following is a clarification of the term directed graph introduced in Example 4.1.7 on p.67 of .

definition.p: A directed graph (or digraph) is a pair G=(V,E) where V is a set and E\subseteq V\times V. An element of V is called a vertex or a node and an element of E is called an edge.

section.p: Given a relation R on a set A, the pair G=(A,R) is a digraph. In a drawing, elements of A are depicted as dots, and (a,a') in R is depicted by a segment of a line or an arc that starts at a, ends at a', and has an arrow pointing from a to a'.
The following is an alternative to Definition 4.4.1 on p.84 of .

definition.p: A (simple) graph is a pair G=(V,E), where V is a set and E is a set of subsets of V, where every element of E contains exactly two elements of V. An element of V is called a vertex or a node and an element of E is called an edge.

section.p: Note on terminology: The plural of vertex is vertices.

Note on an important and almost hidden assumption in . In general, graphs and digraphs may be finite or infinite. In Remark 1 on p.84, Schumacher says that all graphs in this section are assumed to be finite. Without this assumption, some of the theorems are false.
